perturbation.original_text: Either Party may terminate this agreement for non-cause with a sixty (60) written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Either Party may terminate this agreement for non-cause with a ten (10) day written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the termination notice period from 60 days to 10 days could be problematic depending on jurisdiction. Many jurisdictions require a reasonable notice period which may be longer than 10 days depending on the nature of the agreement.
perturbation.law_citation: UCC § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text shortens the notice period for termination to 10 days. While the UCC applies primarily to the sale of goods, its principles regarding reasonable notice for termination are often applied to other contracts. Section 2-309(3) states that termination of a contract by one party requires reasonable notification be received by the other party. Ten days may be deemed an unreasonably short notice period, especially considering the existing relationships and potential losses as a result of a sudden termination.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the termination notice period from 60 days to 10 days, which creates a potential contradiction with UCC principles. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically § 2-309(3), states that termination of a contract requires 'reasonable notification' be received by the other party. The law explanation suggests that 10 days might be considered unreasonable, thus directly contradicting the requirement for reasonable notification as dictated by UCC principles. Therefore, the change introduces an inconsistency with established legal standards for contract termination.

perturbation.original_text: Referral Fees will be paid within thirty (30) days of the collection of receivables from the Registered Referral.
perturbation.changed_text: Referral Fees will be paid within ninety (90) days of the collection of receivables from the Registered Referral.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the payment period for referral fees to 90 days may violate state wage payment laws, particularly if these fees are considered earned wages. States often have laws requiring prompt payment of earned commissions.
perturbation.law_citation: § 40.1-29. Payment of wage; how often; agreement for payment other than monthly; withholding for wage taxes; current work record; posting notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text extends the payment timeframe for referral fees to 90 days after collection. Virginia law, specifically § 40.1-29, mandates the timely payment of wages. While referral fees might not be strictly defined as 'wages,' extending the payment period to 90 days increases the risk of violating the spirit and potentially the letter of the law, especially if these fees are a significant portion of the Reseller's compensation, thereby unduly delaying their payment.

Time and medium of payment; withholding wages; written statement of earnings; agreement for forfeiture of wages; proceedings to enforce compliance; penalties. A. All employers operating a business or engaging an individual to perform domestic service shall establish regular pay periods and rates of pay for employees except executive personnel. All such employers shall pay salaried employees at least once each month and employees paid on an hourly rate at least once every two weeks or twice in each month, except that (i) a student who is currently enrolled in a work-study program or its equivalent administered by any secondary school, institution of higher education, or trade school, and (ii) employees whose weekly wages total more than 150 percent of the average weekly wage of the Commonwealth as defined in § 65.2-500 , upon agreement by each affected employee, may
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment period for referral fees to 90 days, while the original text stipulated 30 days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this could violate state wage payment laws. The Law Explanation specifies Virginia law § 40.1-29, which mandates the timely payment of wages. Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 40.1-29, requires employers to establish regular pay periods and pay employees at least once every two weeks or twice in each month. Although referral fees aren't explicitly 'wages', extending the payment period to 90 days could be seen as a violation of the law's intent to ensure timely payment, especially if the fees are a significant portion of the compensation. This delay contradicts the prompt payment requirement.

perturbation.original_text: For a Registered Opportunity where the customer purchases from Reseller, payment will be made by Reseller to Company within 5 days of receipt of payment (paid when paid).
perturbation.changed_text: For a Registered Opportunity where the customer purchases from Reseller, payment will be made by Reseller to Company within 30 days of receipt of payment (paid when paid).
perturbation.explanation: Extending the payment timeframe to 30 days after reseller receipt of payment would affect the cash flow and possibly contravene prompt payment regulations. Although a contract exists, such delayed payment might violate implicit fair dealing if the company is a small business depending on rapid receipt.
perturbation.law_citation: 31 U.S. Code § 3903 - Interest penalties for late payments
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification extends the payment timeframe to 30 days. While this agreement is between private entities, prompt payment principles are often invoked to ensure fair business practices. The Prompt Payment Act outlines requirements for federal agencies to pay contractors in a timely manner, and analogous state laws exist to protect subcontractors and smaller businesses. The original 5-day period aligns with ensuring timely payments; the new 30-day period could be deemed unreasonable, especially if it negatively impacts the Company's ability to meet its own obligations.

perturbation.justification: The modification from a 5-day payment timeframe to 30 days introduces a potential inconsistency with prompt payment principles. The Prompt Payment Act, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1 (31 USC 3903), emphasizes regulations to carry out timely payments, specifying payment dates or a 30-day limit after invoice receipt. While this act directly applies to federal agencies, the Law Explanation suggests analogous state laws exist to protect smaller businesses. Extending the payment period to 30 days, especially if the original 5-day period was crucial for the Company's financial stability, could be deemed unreasonable and contradict the intent of ensuring timely payments, creating a legal contradiction.
